question: Explain the box model and box-sizing.
answer:
Every element is a box: content → padding → border → margin. By default (content-box) width applies to content only, so padding/border add to the total size.

box-sizing: border-box makes width include padding and border — far more predictable, which is why most resets set it globally.
tip: * { box-sizing: border-box } is the first line of almost every CSS reset.
code: * { box-sizing: border-box; }
deep:
The four layers, inside out

  • Content — the text/image; its size is width × height.
  • Padding — space inside the border, takes the background.
  • Border — the edge around the padding.
  • Margin — transparent space outside, separating this box from others.

content-box vs border-box — the whole point

.a { box-sizing: content-box; width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 5px solid; }
/* rendered width = 200 + 20*2 + 5*2 = 250px  */

.b { box-sizing: border-box; width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 5px solid; }
/* rendered width = 200px  ✅ padding & border fit INSIDE */

With the default content-box, padding and border are added to your declared width, so a "200px" box is actually wider. border-box makes width the final rendered width — which is why nearly every reset sets it globally.

*, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box; }

Margin doesn't count toward width — and it collapses

Margin is always outside the box and never part of width. Vertical margins between block elements also collapse: adjacent 20px and 30px margins produce a 30px gap, not 50px (padding and border never collapse).

Handy diagnostics

  • outline draws like a border but takes no space — great for debugging layout.
  • DevTools' box-model panel shows the four layers with live numbers.

How to say it in an interview

“Every element is a box of content, padding, border, and margin. By default width only sizes the content, so padding and border add to the total — I set box-sizing: border-box globally so width is the actual rendered width. Margin stays outside the box and collapses vertically.”

question: When should you use Flexbox instead of CSS Grid?
answer: Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout system that arranges items in a single row or column. It is ideal for navigation bars, toolbars, buttons, and aligning or distributing items along one axis. CSS Grid is a two-dimensional layout system that manages both rows and columns simultaneously, making it the preferred choice for page layouts, dashboards, galleries, and complex responsive designs.
tip: Remember: Flexbox = one dimension, Grid = two dimensions. Use Flexbox for components and Grid for page layouts.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question evaluates whether you understand the strengths of Flexbox and CSS Grid and can choose the appropriate layout system for different UI requirements.

Flexbox vs Grid

FlexboxCSS Grid
One-dimensional layout (row or column).Two-dimensional layout (rows and columns).
Best for aligning and distributing items.Best for creating complete page layouts.
Content-driven layout.Layout-driven design.
Excellent for navigation bars, forms, menus, and cards.Excellent for dashboards, galleries, and responsive page structures.
Uses properties like justify-content, align-items, and flex-wrap.Uses grid-template-columns, grid-template-rows, grid-area, and gap.

Example

/* Navigation */
.nav {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between;
  align-items: center;
}

/* Product Grid */
.products {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr));
  gap: 20px;
}

Real-world Examples

  • Use Flexbox for navigation bars, headers, footers, toolbars, button groups, and form controls.
  • Use Grid for dashboards, admin panels, image galleries, pricing cards, and full-page layouts.
  • Many modern applications combine both—Grid for the overall layout and Flexbox inside individual components.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Can Flexbox create grid-like layouts?
  • Can Grid replace Flexbox completely?
  • What is the difference between auto-fill and auto-fit?
  • How does fr work in CSS Grid?
  • When would you nest Flexbox inside Grid?

Common Mistakes

  • Using Flexbox for complex two-dimensional page layouts.
  • Using Grid for simple horizontal or vertical alignment.
  • Ignoring gap and relying on margins for spacing.
  • Not combining Grid and Flexbox where each is most suitable.

How to say it in an interview

"I use Flexbox for one-dimensional layouts where I need to align items in a row or column, such as navigation bars, forms, or button groups. I use CSS Grid for two-dimensional layouts involving both rows and columns, such as dashboards or responsive card layouts. In real projects, I often combine them by using Grid for the overall page structure and Flexbox within individual components."

question: How does CSS specificity work?
answer: When multiple CSS rules match the same element, the browser decides which one to apply using the cascade and specificity. Specificity is calculated in the order: Inline styles > IDs > Classes, attributes & pseudo-classes > Elements & pseudo-elements. If two selectors have the same specificity, the rule that appears later in the stylesheet wins. Although !important overrides normal specificity, it should generally be avoided because it makes CSS harder to maintain.
tip: If a style isn't being applied, check the browser's DevTools to inspect which selector has the highest specificity.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests whether you understand how the browser resolves conflicting CSS rules using the cascade, specificity, source order, and !important.

Specificity Hierarchy

SelectorSpecificity
Inline styles1,0,0,0
ID selector (#id)0,1,0,0
Class, attribute, pseudo-class (.class, [type], :hover)0,0,1,0
Element and pseudo-element (div, p, ::before)0,0,0,1

How the Browser Chooses a Rule

  1. Check whether a rule uses !important.
  2. Compare selector specificity.
  3. If specificity is equal, the rule that appears later in the stylesheet wins.

Example

#title {
  color: blue;
}

.card .title {
  color: red;
}

h1 {
  color: green;
}

The element with id="title" will be blue because the ID selector has the highest specificity.

Real-world Examples

  • Overriding third-party library styles.
  • Debugging why a CSS rule is not applied.
  • Styling Angular or React components.
  • Avoiding unnecessary use of !important.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • What is the CSS cascade?
  • What happens when two selectors have the same specificity?
  • Why is !important discouraged?
  • Does the universal selector (*) contribute to specificity?
  • How do pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements affect specificity?

Common Mistakes

  • Using multiple IDs or !important to solve CSS conflicts.
  • Ignoring source order when specificity is equal.
  • Writing overly specific selectors that are difficult to override.

How to say it in an interview

"When multiple CSS rules target the same element, the browser first considers !important, then compares selector specificity, and finally uses source order if the specificity is the same. The specificity order is Inline styles > IDs > Classes/Attributes/Pseudo-classes > Elements/Pseudo-elements. I avoid using !important because it makes CSS harder to maintain."

question: Explain the difference between position: relative, absolute, fixed, and sticky.
answer: The position property controls how an element is placed in the document. relative keeps the element in the normal document flow while allowing it to be offset from its original position. absolute removes the element from the normal flow and positions it relative to its nearest positioned ancestor. fixed positions the element relative to the viewport, so it stays in the same place even while scrolling. sticky behaves like relative until a specified scroll threshold (such as top: 0) is reached, after which it behaves like fixed within its scrolling container.
tip: Remember: Relative = reference, Absolute = nearest positioned parent, Fixed = viewport, Sticky = sticks while scrolling.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question evaluates your understanding of CSS positioning, document flow, containing blocks, and common UI layouts.

Position Types

PositionBehaviorTypical Use
relativeRemains in the normal document flow and can be offset from its original position. Also creates a positioning context for absolutely positioned children.Parent container, small element adjustments.
absoluteRemoved from the normal flow and positioned relative to the nearest ancestor with a position other than static.Badges, tooltips, dropdowns, overlays.
fixedRemoved from the flow and positioned relative to the viewport.Floating action buttons, chat widgets, fixed headers.
stickyActs like relative until a scroll threshold is reached, then sticks within its scroll container.Sticky headers, sidebars, table headings.

Example

.card {
  position: relative;
}

.badge {
  position: absolute;
  top: 8px;
  right: 8px;
}

.header {
  position: sticky;
  top: 0;
}

Real-world Examples

  • Use relative on a card so an absolute badge can be positioned inside it.
  • Use absolute for notification badges, tooltips, and dropdown menus.
  • Use fixed for chat buttons, back-to-top buttons, and persistent navigation.
  • Use sticky for navigation bars, table headers, and sidebar menus that remain visible while scrolling.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • What is the default value of position?
  • What happens if an absolutely positioned element has no positioned ancestor?
  • Why isn't position: sticky working?
  • What is the difference between fixed and sticky?
  • How does z-index interact with positioned elements?

Common Mistakes

  • Forgetting to set position: relative on the parent before using position: absolute.
  • Expecting sticky to work without specifying top, left, right, or bottom.
  • Using fixed when sticky provides a better scrolling experience.
  • Ignoring stacking context issues when combining positioned elements with z-index.

How to say it in an interview

"I use position: relative to establish a positioning context for child elements. position: absolute is used for overlays, badges, and dropdowns because it positions elements relative to the nearest positioned ancestor. position: fixed is ideal for elements that should remain visible while scrolling, such as floating buttons. position: sticky behaves like a normal element until it reaches a specified offset, after which it sticks during scrolling, making it perfect for headers and navigation bars."

question: What is the difference between px, em, rem, %, vh, and vw in CSS?
answer: CSS provides different units for sizing elements. px is a fixed pixel unit. em is relative to the font size of its parent (or the element itself for certain properties). rem is relative to the root (html) font size. % is relative to the size of the parent element. vh and vw represent 1% of the viewport's height and width respectively, making them useful for responsive layouts.
tip: Use rem for typography and spacing, % for fluid layouts, and vh/vw for viewport-based sizing. Avoid excessive use of em unless nested scaling is intended.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of CSS sizing units, responsive design, accessibility, and when each unit should be used in real-world applications.

CSS Units Comparison

UnitRelative ToTypical Use
pxFixed CSS pixels.Borders, shadows, fine-grained positioning.
emParent's font size (or current element for some properties).Components that scale with their parent.
remRoot (html) font size.Typography, spacing, padding, margins.
%Parent element's size.Fluid widths and responsive layouts.
vh1% of viewport height.Hero sections, fullscreen layouts.
vw1% of viewport width.Responsive widths and typography.

Example

.container {
  width: 80%;
  padding: 2rem;
}

h1 {
  font-size: 2rem;
}

.card {
  width: 50vw;
  height: 40vh;
}

Real-world Examples

  • Use rem for consistent typography across the application.
  • Use % for responsive containers.
  • Use vh for landing pages that fill the screen.
  • Use vw for responsive banners or dynamic font sizing.
  • Use px for borders, icons, and hairline elements.

Common Mistakes

  • Using em everywhere can lead to unexpected size compounding.
  • Using only px reduces scalability and accessibility.
  • Using 100vh on mobile may not account for browser UI, causing layout issues.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Why is rem generally preferred over em?
  • What causes em values to compound?
  • When would you choose vh over %?
  • What are the issues with using 100vh on mobile browsers?

How to say it in an interview

"I typically use rem for fonts and spacing because it's predictable and respects the user's root font size. I use percentages for flexible layouts, vh and vw for viewport-based sizing, and reserve px for fixed values like borders or shadows. I use em only when I intentionally want sizing to scale relative to its parent."

question: How do you center an element?
answer:
Modern answer: flexbox or grid.

  • Flex: display:flex; justify-content:center; align-items:center;
  • Grid: display:grid; place-items:center;
  • Absolute: top:50%; left:50%; transform:translate(-50%,-50%);
tip: place-items: center is the shortest way to center in both axes.
code: .box { display: grid; place-items: center; }

question: What is the difference between a pseudo-class and a pseudo-element?
answer: A pseudo-class selects an existing element based on its state or position, such as :hover, :focus, or :nth-child(). A pseudo-element selects or creates a specific part of an element, such as ::before, ::after, ::first-line, or ::placeholder.
tip: Think of it this way: pseudo-classes change how an existing element behaves, while pseudo-elements create or target part of an element.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests whether you understand the difference between selecting an element's state and selecting or creating a part of an element. Interviewers often ask for examples of each.

Pseudo-class vs Pseudo-element

Pseudo-classPseudo-element
Selects an existing element based on its state or position.Selects or creates a specific part of an element.
Uses a single colon (:).Uses two colons (::).
Examples: :hover, :focus, :active, :checked, :nth-child().Examples: ::before, ::after, ::first-letter, ::first-line, ::placeholder.

Example

button:hover {
  background: blue;
}

.card::before {
  content: "New";
  color: red;
}

Real-world Examples

  • Use :hover to change a button's appearance.
  • Use :focus to highlight an active input.
  • Use :nth-child() to style alternating table rows.
  • Use ::before or ::after to add icons, badges, or decorative elements without extra HTML.
  • Use ::placeholder to style placeholder text.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Why do pseudo-elements require the content property with ::before and ::after?
  • Can you attach event listeners to pseudo-elements?
  • Why are pseudo-elements useful for reducing unnecessary HTML?
  • Why do modern CSS specifications recommend using double colons for pseudo-elements?

Common Mistakes

  • Confusing :hover with ::before.
  • Forgetting to specify the content property when using ::before or ::after.
  • Adding unnecessary HTML instead of using pseudo-elements for decorative content.

How to say it in an interview

"A pseudo-class styles an existing element based on its state or position, such as :hover or :focus. A pseudo-element styles or creates a specific part of an element, such as ::before or ::after. I commonly use pseudo-classes for interactive states and pseudo-elements for icons, badges, and decorative content without adding extra HTML."

question: What are CSS custom properties (CSS variables)?
answer: CSS custom properties (also called CSS variables) are reusable values defined using the -- prefix and accessed with the var() function. Unlike preprocessor variables (Sass/Less), CSS variables exist at runtime, participate in the CSS cascade, inherit by default, and can be updated dynamically using JavaScript. They are commonly used for theming, design systems, and reducing duplicated values.
tip: Define global variables in :root and override them for themes, such as [data-theme="dark"], without changing component styles.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question evaluates your understanding of CSS custom properties, the CSS cascade, inheritance, dynamic styling, and their advantages over preprocessor variables.

What Are CSS Variables?

CSS custom properties are variables declared using the -- prefix. Their values are accessed using the var() function and are resolved by the browser at runtime.

Syntax

:root {
  --primary-color: #2563eb;
}

.button {
  color: var(--primary-color);
}

Why Use CSS Variables?

  • Reduce duplicated values.
  • Create consistent design systems.
  • Implement light and dark themes.
  • Update styles dynamically using JavaScript.
  • Take advantage of inheritance and the CSS cascade.

CSS Variables vs Sass/Less Variables

CSS VariablesSass/Less Variables
Evaluated at runtime.Compiled at build time.
Can be modified with JavaScript.Cannot be changed after compilation.
Participate in the CSS cascade and inheritance.No runtime cascade or inheritance.
Ideal for themes and dynamic styling.Ideal for static values and code organization.

Real-world Examples

  • Light and dark mode switching.
  • Brand color customization.
  • Shared spacing and typography tokens.
  • Reusable border radius and shadow values.
  • Enterprise design systems.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How are CSS variables different from Sass variables?
  • Why are CSS variables useful for theming?
  • Can CSS variables be changed with JavaScript?
  • Where should global variables be declared?
  • What happens if a CSS variable is undefined?

Common Mistakes

  • Defining global variables outside :root unnecessarily.
  • Using hard-coded values instead of reusable variables.
  • Confusing CSS variables with Sass or Less variables.
  • Forgetting that CSS variables follow the normal cascade and inheritance rules.

How to say it in an interview

"CSS custom properties are runtime variables declared using the -- syntax and accessed with var(). Unlike Sass variables, they participate in the CSS cascade, inherit naturally, and can be updated dynamically with JavaScript. I commonly use them to build design systems and implement light and dark themes."

question: How do you build a responsive layout in CSS?
answer: A responsive layout adapts to different screen sizes and devices. Use a mobile-first approach by writing base styles for smaller screens, then enhance the layout using min-width media queries. Combine flexible layouts like Flexbox and CSS Grid with fluid units such as %, rem, fr, and functions like minmax(). Also include the viewport meta tag so layouts scale correctly on mobile devices.
tip: Build mobile-first. Modern CSS Grid using repeat(auto-fit, minmax()) often creates responsive layouts without many media queries.
code:
<!-- HTML -->
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">

/* CSS */
.cards {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr));
  gap: 16px;
}

@media (min-width: 768px) {
  .sidebar {
    display: block;
  }
}
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of responsive web design, modern CSS layout techniques, mobile-first development, and how websites adapt across different screen sizes.

Best Practices for Responsive Design

  • Use a mobile-first approach.
  • Include the viewport meta tag.
  • Use flexible layouts with Flexbox or CSS Grid.
  • Prefer fluid units like %, rem, fr, and vw.
  • Use media queries only when the layout actually needs to change.
  • Make images responsive using max-width: 100%.

Common Responsive Techniques

TechniquePurpose
FlexboxResponsive one-dimensional layouts.
CSS GridResponsive two-dimensional layouts.
repeat(auto-fit, minmax())Automatically adjusts the number of columns.
Media QueriesApply styles at specific breakpoints.
Fluid UnitsScale elements naturally across devices.
Responsive ImagesPrevent images from overflowing their containers.

Example

.cards {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr));
  gap: 16px;
}

img {
  max-width: 100%;
  height: auto;
}

@media (min-width: 768px) {
  .sidebar {
    display: block;
  }
}

Real-world Examples

  • Product listing pages that automatically adjust the number of columns.
  • Navigation menus that collapse into a hamburger menu on mobile.
  • Responsive dashboards with sidebars that appear only on larger screens.
  • Image galleries that resize without overflowing.
  • Landing pages optimized for phones, tablets, and desktops.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • What is mobile-first design?
  • Why use min-width instead of max-width media queries?
  • What is the purpose of the viewport meta tag?
  • What is the difference between auto-fit and auto-fill?
  • When can CSS Grid eliminate the need for media queries?

Common Mistakes

  • Using fixed widths instead of flexible layouts.
  • Creating too many breakpoint-specific media queries.
  • Forgetting the viewport meta tag.
  • Using pixels everywhere instead of responsive units.
  • Not testing layouts on different screen sizes.

How to say it in an interview

"I build responsive layouts using a mobile-first approach. I use Flexbox and CSS Grid with flexible units like fr, %, and rem. I rely on features like repeat(auto-fit, minmax()) to create adaptive layouts and add min-width media queries only when the layout needs to change. I also include the viewport meta tag to ensure proper rendering on mobile devices."

question: What are CSS combinators? Explain the descendant, child, adjacent sibling, and general sibling combinators.
answer: CSS combinators define the relationship between selectors. The descendant combinator (A B) selects any matching descendant. The child combinator (A > B) selects only direct children. The adjacent sibling combinator (A + B) selects the immediate next sibling. The general sibling combinator (A ~ B) selects all following siblings that share the same parent.
tip: Remember: Space = any descendant, > = direct child, + = next sibling, ~ = all following siblings.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests whether you understand CSS selector relationships and know how to target elements efficiently without adding unnecessary classes or IDs.

CSS Combinators

CombinatorDescriptionExample
A BSelects all matching descendants at any nesting level..card p
A > BSelects only direct children..card > p
A + BSelects the immediate next sibling.h2 + p
A ~ BSelects all following siblings with the same parent.h2 ~ p

Example

<div class="card">
  <p>Direct child</p>
  <section>
    <p>Nested paragraph</p>
  </section>
</div>
  • .card p selects both paragraphs.
  • .card > p selects only the first paragraph.

Real-world Examples

  • Style navigation links that are direct children of a menu.
  • Remove the top margin from the paragraph immediately following a heading using h2 + p.
  • Highlight all messages that come after a selected message using ~.
  • Scope component styles using > to avoid affecting deeply nested elements.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • What is the difference between A B and A > B?
  • When would you use + instead of ~?
  • Do combinators affect CSS specificity?
  • Can combinators be combined with pseudo-classes?
  • How do combinators help write maintainable CSS?

Common Mistakes

  • Using the descendant selector when only direct children should be styled.
  • Confusing + (next sibling) with ~ (all following siblings).
  • Writing overly broad descendant selectors that unintentionally style nested elements.

How to say it in an interview

"CSS combinators define relationships between elements. A space selects any descendant, > selects only direct children, + selects the immediate next sibling, and ~ selects all following siblings with the same parent. I prefer using the most specific combinator, especially >, to keep component styles predictable and avoid unintended styling."

question: Why doesn't my z-index work? (stacking context)
answer: z-index only compares elements within the same stacking context. Properties like transform, opacity < 1, filter, and position + z-index create new contexts, trapping children's z-index inside them.
tip: A parent's transform can make a huge child z-index useless — check ancestors first.

question: Which CSS properties are best for smooth animations?
answer: For smooth, high-performance animations, prefer animating transform and opacity. These properties are handled by the browser's compositor and typically avoid layout recalculations and repainting. Avoid animating layout-related properties such as width, height, top, left, and margin, as they trigger layout updates and can cause janky animations.
tip: The golden rule is: Animate only transform and opacity whenever possible for smooth 60 FPS animations.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of browser rendering, animation performance, and which CSS properties minimize rendering work.

Browser Rendering Pipeline

When rendering a page, the browser generally performs these steps:

  1. Layout (Reflow) – Calculates element positions and sizes.
  2. Paint – Draws pixels for elements.
  3. Composite – Combines layers and displays them on screen.

Animations that skip Layout and Paint are significantly smoother.

Performance Comparison

PropertyPerformanceReason
transform★★★★★Handled by the compositor without layout changes.
opacity★★★★★Only changes transparency, no layout required.
width, height★★☆☆☆Triggers layout and repaint.
top, left★★☆☆☆Changes element position, causing layout.
margin, padding★★☆☆☆Requires layout recalculation.
background-color★★★☆☆Causes repaint but not layout.

Example

.card {
  transition: transform .2s ease;
}

.card:hover {
  transform: translateY(-4px) scale(1.02);
}

Real-world Examples

  • Hover effects on cards and buttons.
  • Sliding side navigation menus.
  • Modal fade-in and fade-out animations.
  • Image zoom effects.
  • Loading skeletons and shimmer animations.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Why is transform faster than top or left?
  • What is the difference between reflow, repaint, and compositing?
  • What is GPU compositing?
  • When would you use will-change?
  • How do you identify slow animations using Chrome DevTools?

Common Mistakes

  • Animating width or height for expand/collapse effects.
  • Moving elements using top and left instead of transform: translate().
  • Applying will-change to too many elements, which can increase memory usage.

How to say it in an interview

"For smooth animations, I always try to animate transform and opacity because they are handled by the browser's compositor and usually avoid layout and repaint operations. I avoid animating properties like width, height, or top since they trigger reflow and can reduce animation performance."

question: What is BEM, and why is it used in CSS?
answer: BEM (Block, Element, Modifier) is a CSS naming convention that makes class names predictable, reusable, and easy to maintain. It promotes low-specificity selectors, avoids deeply nested CSS, and makes components easier to understand and modify. BEM is especially useful in large projects and teams because it reduces style conflicts and improves code organization.
tip: Think of BEM as: Block = component, Element = part of the component, Modifier = variation of the component.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests whether you understand CSS architecture, maintainable naming conventions, and how to organize styles in large-scale applications.

What is BEM?

BEM stands for Block, Element, and Modifier. It is a naming convention that encourages reusable, component-based CSS with low specificity.

PartMeaningExample
BlockA standalone component..card
ElementA child that belongs to the block..card__title
ModifierA variation or state of a block or element..card--featured, .card__button--disabled

Example

<div class="card card--featured">
  <img class="card__image">
  <h2 class="card__title">Product</h2>
  <button class="card__button card__button--disabled">
    Buy Now
  </button>
</div>

Why Use BEM?

  • Keeps CSS predictable and easy to understand.
  • Reduces selector specificity by relying on class selectors.
  • Avoids deeply nested selectors.
  • Prevents accidental style overrides.
  • Makes components reusable and easier to maintain.
  • Works well with Angular, React, Vue, and other component-based frameworks.

Real-world Examples

  • Large enterprise applications with hundreds of reusable components.
  • Design systems and component libraries.
  • Teams where multiple developers work on the same stylesheet.
  • Projects requiring consistent naming across modules.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • Why is BEM better than deeply nested selectors?
  • Does BEM improve CSS performance?
  • How does BEM help reduce specificity issues?
  • How is BEM different from CSS Modules or Tailwind CSS?
  • Is BEM still useful in Angular or React projects?

Common Mistakes

  • Creating unnecessary nesting like .card .card__title instead of using the BEM class directly.
  • Using IDs together with BEM classes, increasing specificity.
  • Treating modifiers as separate components instead of variations.
  • Creating overly long or inconsistent class names.

How to say it in an interview

"BEM is a CSS naming convention that stands for Block, Element, and Modifier. It helps organize styles into reusable components with low specificity, making CSS easier to maintain and less prone to conflicts. I've used BEM in Angular projects because it keeps component styles predictable and works well in large codebases with multiple developers."

question: Explain flex-grow, flex-shrink, and flex-basis.
answer:
The flex shorthand controls how items share space:

  • flex-grow — how much an item grows to fill free space.
  • flex-shrink — how much it shrinks when space is tight.
  • flex-basis — the starting size before grow/shrink.

flex: 1 = 1 1 0 (grow equally from zero).
tip: flex: 1 on children makes them share the row equally — the most-used flex value.
code: .col { flex: 1 1 200px; } /* grow, shrink, basis */
deep:
Two axes: main and cross

Flexbox is one-dimensional. flex-direction sets the main axis (row = horizontal, column = vertical); the cross axis is perpendicular. All the alignment properties are defined relative to these axes, which is the thing to get straight first.

PropertyAxisDoes
justify-contentmaindistributes items along the main axis
align-itemscrossaligns items on the cross axis
align-contentcrossspaces multiple lines (when wrapped)
align-selfcrossoverrides align-items for one item

The three flex-item properties

  • flex-grow — a unitless ratio of how leftover free space is shared. 0 = don't grow. Two items at 1 and 2 split extra space 1:2.
  • flex-shrink — ratio for how items give up space when the container is too small. 0 = never shrink (can overflow).
  • flex-basis — the item's size along the main axis before grow/shrink. auto uses the content/width; 0 ignores it.

Reading the flex shorthand

flex: 1;        /* 1 1 0   — grow & shrink from zero, equal columns */
flex: auto;     /* 1 1 auto — grow/shrink from content size */
flex: none;     /* 0 0 auto — fixed, never grow or shrink */
flex: 0 0 200px;/* fixed 200px track */

Key nuance: flex: 1 uses basis 0, so items become truly equal regardless of content. flex: auto uses basis auto, so bigger content starts bigger. That difference trips people up constantly.

Common patterns

/* perfect centering */
.center { display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }

/* push last item to the right (nav bar) */
.nav { display: flex; gap: 12px; }
.nav .spacer { margin-left: auto; }

/* responsive wrap */
.cards { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 16px; }
.cards > * { flex: 1 1 240px; } /* grow, but never below ~240px */

Gotchas

  • Items overflow instead of shrinking? A child's min-width defaults to auto (min-content) — set min-width: 0 to let it shrink.
  • margin: auto on a flex item absorbs free space — handy for pushing items apart.
  • Use gap for spacing, not margins — it's cleaner and doesn't add edge gaps.

How to say it in an interview

“Flexbox lays items along a main axis set by flex-direction; justify-content aligns on the main axis and align-items on the cross axis. On items, flex-grow/shrink are ratios for sharing or giving up space and flex-basis is the starting size — flex: 1 means grow and shrink from a zero basis, giving equal columns.”

question: What is grid-template-areas and when is it used?
answer: grid-template-areas lets you define named regions in a CSS Grid using a readable, text-based layout. Child elements are assigned to these regions using the grid-area property. This makes complex layouts easier to understand, maintain, and rearrange for responsive designs by simply changing the area definitions.
tip: Use grid-template-areas for page layouts with clearly defined sections like headers, sidebars, and content. Responsive layouts often require changing only the area map.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of advanced CSS Grid features, readable layout design, and responsive page architecture.

How grid-template-areas Works

Instead of placing items using row and column numbers, you assign names to sections of the grid. Each child element is then placed into its named area using the grid-area property.

Example

.layout {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 250px 1fr;
  grid-template-areas:
    "header header"
    "sidebar main"
    "footer footer";
}

.header { grid-area: header; }
.sidebar { grid-area: sidebar; }
.main { grid-area: main; }
.footer { grid-area: footer; }

Responsive Example

Instead of changing the HTML, you can simply redefine the layout:

@media (max-width: 768px) {
  .layout {
    grid-template-areas:
      "header"
      "main"
      "sidebar"
      "footer";
  }
}

Benefits

  • Makes layouts easy to read and understand.
  • Separates layout structure from HTML.
  • Responsive layouts require minimal CSS changes.
  • Avoids manually managing grid row and column numbers.
  • Ideal for dashboards, admin panels, and page templates.

Real-world Examples

  • Blog layouts with a header, sidebar, content, and footer.
  • Admin dashboards.
  • E-commerce product pages.
  • Documentation websites.
  • News portals with multiple content sections.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • What is the difference between grid-template-areas and manually placing items with grid lines?
  • Can one area span multiple rows or columns?
  • Can an area be left empty?
  • How do you make grid-template-areas responsive?
  • When should you use Grid Areas instead of Flexbox?

Common Mistakes

  • Using different area names in grid-template-areas and grid-area.
  • Creating rows with different numbers of area names.
  • Trying to use Grid Areas for simple one-dimensional layouts where Flexbox is more appropriate.
  • Changing HTML order unnecessarily instead of redefining the grid areas.

How to say it in an interview

"grid-template-areas allows me to define named sections of a CSS Grid using a visual layout. I assign elements to those regions with grid-area, making complex layouts much easier to read and maintain. For responsive designs, I simply redefine the area map inside a media query instead of changing the HTML or manually repositioning elements."

question: What is clamp() and when is it used?
answer: The clamp() function lets you define a responsive value by specifying a minimum, a preferred, and a maximum value. The browser uses the preferred value as long as it stays within the specified range. It is commonly used for fluid typography, spacing, widths, and other responsive values without relying on multiple media queries.
tip: A common pattern is clamp(min, viewport-based value, max), which creates smooth responsive sizing while preventing values from becoming too small or too large.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of modern responsive CSS techniques and whether you know how to create fluid layouts without excessive media queries.

Syntax

clamp(minimum, preferred, maximum)

The browser calculates the preferred value and ensures it never goes below the minimum or above the maximum.

ParameterPurpose
MinimumThe smallest allowed value.
PreferredUsually a responsive value such as vw.
MaximumThe largest allowed value.

Example

h1 {
  font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 4vw, 3rem);
}

.card {
  padding: clamp(1rem, 2vw, 2rem);
}

As the viewport grows, the font size and padding increase smoothly but never exceed their maximum values or shrink below their minimum values.

Why Use clamp()?

  • Creates fluid typography.
  • Reduces the need for multiple media queries.
  • Improves responsiveness with less CSS.
  • Keeps layouts readable on both small and large screens.
  • Works well for spacing, widths, margins, and gaps.

Real-world Examples

  • Responsive headings on landing pages.
  • Adaptive padding and margins.
  • Fluid button sizes.
  • Responsive card layouts.
  • Design systems using scalable spacing tokens.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • How is clamp() different from min() and max()?
  • Why is vw commonly used as the preferred value?
  • Can clamp() replace media queries?
  • When would you still use media queries?
  • What CSS properties can use clamp()?

Common Mistakes

  • Using fixed pixel values for all three arguments, making the value non-responsive.
  • Choosing unrealistic minimum or maximum values.
  • Assuming clamp() completely replaces media queries—it handles sizing, not layout changes.
  • Forgetting that the preferred value should usually be viewport-relative (for example, vw).

How to say it in an interview

"The clamp() function creates responsive values by specifying a minimum, preferred, and maximum value. The browser uses the preferred value as long as it stays within the defined range. I commonly use it for fluid typography and spacing because it produces smooth scaling across screen sizes while reducing the number of media queries required."

question: How do you keep an element's aspect ratio?
answer: The aspect-ratio property reserves space in a fixed ratio (e.g. 16/9) so the box sizes correctly and avoids layout shift — replacing the old padding-top hack. Great for video/image placeholders.
tip: aspect-ratio prevents cumulative layout shift (CLS) for media before it loads.
code: .video { aspect-ratio: 16 / 9; width: 100%; }

question: What does object-fit do?
answer: Controls how a replaced element (img/video) fills its box: cover (fill, crop), contain (fit, letterbox), fill (stretch). Pair with object-position to control the crop focus.
tip: object-fit: cover is the CSS way to crop images to a fixed box without distortion.
code: .avatar { width: 64px; height: 64px; object-fit: cover; }

question: What are CSS Container Queries, and how do they differ from Media Queries?
answer: Container Queries allow a component to change its styles based on the size of its parent container, whereas Media Queries respond to the size of the viewport. This makes components truly reusable because they adapt to the space available rather than the device size. Container Queries are ideal for building responsive UI components that may appear in sidebars, cards, or full-page layouts.
tip: Think of it this way: Media Queries respond to the screen, Container Queries respond to the component's parent.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question evaluates whether you understand modern responsive design techniques and know the difference between viewport-based and component-based responsiveness.

Media Queries vs Container Queries

Media QueriesContainer Queries
Respond to the viewport size.Respond to the size of a parent container.
Useful for page-level layouts.Ideal for reusable UI components.
All components change together when the screen size changes.Each component adapts independently based on its available space.
Uses @media.Uses @container.

How Container Queries Work

First, declare a container by setting container-type on the parent element. Then use the @container rule to apply styles based on the container's dimensions.

.wrapper {
  container-type: inline-size;
}

@container (min-width: 400px) {
  .card {
    display: flex;
  }
}

Why Use Container Queries?

  • Create truly reusable components.
  • Components adapt to their available space instead of the entire screen.
  • Reduce component-specific media queries.
  • Improve maintainability in design systems.
  • Perfect for cards, widgets, sidebars, and dashboards.

Real-world Examples

  • A product card displayed vertically in a sidebar but horizontally in the main content.
  • Dashboard widgets that resize independently.
  • Reusable components inside different layouts.
  • Responsive card grids without relying on viewport breakpoints.
  • Component libraries used across multiple applications.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • When should you use Container Queries instead of Media Queries?
  • What does container-type: inline-size do?
  • Can Media Queries and Container Queries be used together?
  • What types of containers can be queried?
  • Are Container Queries supported in modern browsers?

Common Mistakes

  • Forgetting to declare a container using container-type.
  • Using Container Queries for page-level layouts where Media Queries are more appropriate.
  • Assuming Container Queries replace Media Queries completely—they complement each other.
  • Applying Container Queries without understanding the parent container's size.

How to say it in an interview

"Media Queries make layouts responsive based on the viewport, while Container Queries make individual components responsive based on the size of their parent container. I use Media Queries for overall page layouts and Container Queries for reusable components like cards, widgets, and sidebars so they can adapt regardless of where they're placed."

question: What is the :has() selector?
answer: The long-awaited parent selector: style an element based on its descendants or following siblings. E.g. style a card that contains an image, or a label whose input is invalid — logic that previously required JavaScript.
tip: :has() finally lets you style a parent based on its children — no JS needed.
code:
.card:has(img) { padding: 0; }
label:has(input:invalid) { color: red; }

question: What are cascade layers (@layer)?
answer: @layer lets you group styles into ordered layers (e.g. reset, base, components, utilities). Layer order wins over specificity, so you can manage the cascade predictably and stop fighting !important in large codebases.
tip: Layers let a low-specificity utility beat a high-specificity component rule intentionally.
code:
@layer reset, base, components;
@layer components { .btn { color: red; } }

question: What are logical properties (margin-inline, inset)?
answer: Direction-aware properties that adapt to writing mode/text direction: margin-inline/block, padding-inline, inset. They make layouts work automatically in RTL languages without separate stylesheets.
tip: Use margin-inline instead of margin-left/right for RTL-ready layouts.
code: .box { margin-inline: auto; padding-block: 1rem; }

question: What does will-change do, and when should you avoid it?
answer: will-change hints the browser to promote an element to its own GPU layer ahead of an animation, reducing jank. But overusing it wastes memory and can hurt performance — apply it sparingly, ideally just before animating, and remove it after.
tip: Don't slap will-change on everything — it's a targeted hint, not a global optimisation.
code: .modal { will-change: transform, opacity; }

question: display:none vs visibility:hidden vs opacity:0 — how do they differ?
answer:
Takes space?Clickable?Screen readers?
display:noneno (removed from layout)nono
visibility:hiddenyes (space reserved)nono
opacity:0yesyes (still there)yes
tip: opacity:0 is invisible but still clickable — a common source of 'ghost' click bugs.
code:
.gone   { display: none; }      /* no space, not rendered */
.hidden { visibility: hidden; } /* space kept, invisible */
.faded  { opacity: 0; }         /* space kept, still interactive */
deep:
The three are not interchangeable

  • display:none — the element is gone from the layout entirely. No box, no space, not focusable, ignored by screen readers. Toggling it reflows the page.
  • visibility:hidden — the element is invisible but still occupies its space (a hole in the layout). Not clickable, not read by AT.
  • opacity:0 — fully transparent but fully present: it takes space, is still clickable/focusable, and is announced by screen readers. Only opacity is animatable of the three.

Which to use

  • Remove from flow (menus, tabs): display:none.
  • Reserve space to avoid layout shift: visibility:hidden.
  • Fade in/out with a transition: animate opacity (add pointer-events:none so the invisible element isn't clickable).
.fade { opacity: 0; pointer-events: none; transition: opacity .2s; }
.fade.show { opacity: 1; pointer-events: auto; }

How to say it in an interview

“display:none removes the element and its space; visibility:hidden hides it but keeps the space; opacity:0 makes it transparent while still taking space and staying interactive — so I add pointer-events:none and use opacity when I want to animate a fade.”

question: What is float, and what problem does clearfix solve?
answer:
float pulls an element to the left/right and lets text/inline content wrap around it — its original purpose (e.g. an image inside a paragraph). Floated elements are taken out of normal flow, so a parent containing only floats collapses to zero height.

clearfix forces the parent to contain its floats. The modern one-liner is display: flow-root; the old trick is a clearing pseudo-element.
tip: For layout, use Flexbox/Grid — float is now mainly for wrapping text around an image.
code:
/* modern */ .parent { display: flow-root; }
/* legacy  */ .clearfix::after { content: ''; display: block; clear: both; }

question: What is the overflow property in CSS?
answer: The overflow property controls what happens when an element's content exceeds its available space. visible lets content overflow outside the element. hidden clips the overflow without showing scrollbars. auto displays scrollbars only when needed. scroll always shows scrollbars, even if there is no overflow. clip clips overflowing content like hidden but completely disables scrolling, including programmatic scrolling.
tip: Use overflow: auto for most scrollable containers. Reserve scroll only when you always want scrollbars visible.
deep:
What the Interviewer Expects

This question tests your understanding of how CSS handles overflowing content, when scrollbars appear, and which overflow value is appropriate for different UI scenarios.

Overflow Values

ValueBehaviorCommon Use
visibleContent is allowed to overflow outside the element.Default behavior.
hiddenOverflow is clipped and no scrollbars are shown.Image cropping, hiding excess content.
autoScrollbars appear only when the content overflows.Scrollable panels, tables, chat windows.
scrollScrollbars are always visible regardless of overflow.Rarely used when a consistent scrollbar is desired.
clipOverflow is clipped and scrolling is completely disabled.Performance-sensitive layouts and decorative clipping.

Example

.chat {
  height: 300px;
  overflow-y: auto;
}

.avatar {
  overflow: hidden;
  border-radius: 50%;
}

pre {
  overflow-x: auto;
}

Real-world Examples

  • Chat applications with vertically scrollable message lists.
  • Scrollable code snippets using overflow-x: auto.
  • Cropping profile images inside circular containers with overflow: hidden.
  • Dashboards with scrollable sidebars.
  • Responsive tables that scroll horizontally on small screens.

Common Interview Follow-ups

  • What is the difference between hidden and clip?
  • What are overflow-x and overflow-y?
  • Why isn't my scrollbar appearing?
  • How do you create horizontal scrolling for tables or code blocks?
  • Can overflow create a new block formatting context?

Common Mistakes

  • Using overflow: scroll when auto provides a better user experience.
  • Expecting scrolling without setting a constrained width or height.
  • Using overflow: hidden and then wondering why content cannot be accessed.
  • Confusing clip with hidden; unlike hidden, clip completely disables scrolling.

How to say it in an interview

"The overflow property controls how content behaves when it exceeds an element's boundaries. visible allows overflow, hidden clips it without scrollbars, auto adds scrollbars only when necessary, scroll always shows scrollbars, and clip clips the content while preventing any scrolling. In most applications, I use overflow: auto because it provides the best user experience."

question: What is the difference between width, min-width and max-width?
answer: width sets the preferred width. min-width prevents the element from becoming smaller than a given size. max-width prevents it from growing beyond a given size. Together they make layouts responsive while avoiding elements becoming too small or too large.
tip: max-width:100% is commonly used to keep images responsive.

question: Why does a Flexbox child sometimes overflow even though flex-shrink is enabled?
answer: Flex items have a default min-width:auto, which prevents them from shrinking smaller than their content. Long text or large elements can therefore overflow. Setting min-width:0 allows the item to shrink correctly.
tip: If text refuses to truncate inside a flex item, the first thing to try is min-width:0.

question: What is the difference between height and min-height?
answer: height fixes the element's height. If the content is larger, it may overflow. min-height sets the minimum height but allows the element to grow if more content is added. It is commonly used for responsive layouts and full-page sections.
tip: Prefer min-height for content containers because it adapts to varying content.

question: What does pointer-events: none do and when should you use it?
answer: pointer-events:none prevents an element from receiving mouse or touch events. Clicks pass through to the element underneath. It is useful for disabled overlays, decorative elements, loading screens, or invisible elements with opacity:0.
tip: When using opacity:0, also consider pointer-events:none to avoid invisible clickable elements.

question: When should you use transform: translate() instead of top/left positioning?
answer: transform: translate() moves an element visually without affecting document layout, making it ideal for animations and transitions. Changing top or left can trigger layout recalculations (reflow), making animations less efficient.
tip: For smooth animations, prefer transform because browsers can optimize it using the GPU.

question: What is the difference between CSS Transition and CSS Animation?
answer: Transition animates between two states when a property changes (for example on hover). Animation uses @keyframes and can run automatically, repeat infinitely, or contain multiple stages without user interaction.
tip: Use Transition for simple state changes and Animation for complex or repeating effects.

question: How do you display an ellipsis (...) when text overflows?
answer: Use the combination of white-space: nowrap, overflow: hidden, and text-overflow: ellipsis. All three properties are required for single-line text truncation.
tip: Many developers forget overflow:hidden, causing ellipsis not to appear.

question: How do you create a triangle using only CSS?
answer: A CSS triangle is created by giving an element zero width and height, then applying transparent borders on three sides and a colored border on the remaining side.
tip: This technique is commonly used for tooltips, dropdown arrows and speech bubbles.

question: What is the difference between display:none, visibility:hidden, opacity:0 and pointer-events:none?
answer:
• display:none removes the element from the layout.
  • visibility:hidden hides the element but still reserves its space.
  • opacity:0 makes the element invisible but it still occupies space and remains clickable.
  • pointer-events:none disables mouse/touch interaction while keeping the element visible (or invisible).
tip: An element with opacity:0 can still receive clicks unless pointer-events:none is also applied.
